birth.place: Mattingley, Hampshire, England (near Hook and Reading, River Whitewater)

death.place: St. Mary's County, Province of Maryland

key_facts: Emigrated with wife and four children from England to Maryland in 1663-1664
Died intestate shortly after arrival; widow Elizabeth was given letters of administration on 24 July 1664
Paid own passage to Maryland entitling family to 50 acres per person (300 acres total)
The Mattingly surname originates in Mattingley village, Hampshire, England (Wikipedia confirmed)
Mattingley means 'Matta's ley' (place of the Matta family) in Old English
Father: Charles Mattingly (abt. 1600-1642, Omny Parish, Sussex, England)
Mother: Judith Bugbee (abt. 1602, England)
Grandfather: John Mattingly (abt. 1560-1641, Berkshire, England)
Grandmother: Francis Mattingly (1580-1620, England)
Wife: Elizabeth McWilliams (abt. 1626 - after 1669)
sources: Herman Mattingly, The Mattingly Family in Early America (1975) — referenced in multiple genealogical profiles

teichmueller_german.summary: Hans Teichmueller (1837-1901) came from a distinguished Brunswick family. His father August was a Brunswick Army officer; his mother Charlotte was born von Gursewald (a noble German surname with the 'von' aristocratic prefix). Hans attended school in Brunswick city and the Gymnasium at Blankenburg (Harz), then emigrated to Texas in 1856 after his father's death. He married Augusta Kellner of Postoak Point (whose father was a languages teacher) on 16 June 1858. Together they had five children: May (died ~1900, wife of Ed Mattingly), Minetta, Anna, and two unnamed children. Hans became District Judge of the 22nd Judicial District, serving from 1884 until his death on 17 February 1901 — one of the most prominent figures in Fayette County history.

mattingly_paternal.summary: The Mattingly surname originates in Mattingley village, Hampshire, England ('Matta's ley' in Old English). Thomas Mattingly I (abt. 1624-1664) emigrated from Sussex/Hampshire, England to Maryland in 1663-64 with wife Elizabeth McWilliams and four children. He died intestate shortly after arrival; his widow remarried attorney Walter Pake who secured the family's 300-acre land claim 'Mattingly's Hope' in Charles County, Maryland (patent 4 September 1666). The line proceeds: Thomas I -> Thomas II (abt. 1650-1715, eight children) -> Thomas III (abt. 1688-1774, sons Leonard/Edward/John Baptist/Clement named in will) -> collateral lines to Maryland Catholic community and eventually Kentucky (1785 migration). The documented English ancestry extends: Thomas I <- Charles Mattingly (1600, Sussex) <- Judith Bugbee; John Mattingly (1560, Berkshire) is Thomas I's grandfather.

baity_maternal.summary: The 'Pearl Baity' of family oral history is documented in records as Paralee Johnson Baity (maiden name Johnson). Her husband William Alexander Baity (1862-1945) was from Courtney, Yadkin County, NC; he moved to north Texas (Denison/Grayson County) by 1899, then to San Antonio by 1910, where he remained until his death on 30 January 1945. Their daughter Ruth (Baity) Mattingly married into the Mattingly family — the direct documented bridge between the two family lines. The Baity ancestry extends back through Yadkin and Surry County NC: William D. Baity (1829-1894) -> Isham Baity (1804-1892) -> David Baity Sr. (1782-1856) -> George Baity Sr. (1746-1828, Revolutionary era). Florence Thornton Butt (HEB founder) had a similar Carolinas/upper South background; the 'childhood friend' claim in oral history is plausible but unverified.

frost_maternal.summary: Frost Bank was founded in San Antonio in 1868 by Thomas Claiborne Frost, a former Confederate lieutenant colonel. His son T.C. Frost Jr. ran the bank until approximately 1926, when his brother Joseph Hardin Frost took over as president. If Shari's mother was born 1923 and her maternal uncle was 'Frost Bank president', the most likely candidate for Shari's grandmother is a sister (or daughter) of T.C. Frost Jr. and Joseph Hardin Frost. This would make Shari's grandmother a child of Thomas Claiborne Frost (founder). The specific identity of this grandmother — her name and birth/death dates — remains unconfirmed and requires asking Shari directly or searching San Antonio social records.

open_questions: Which specific Mattingly did Ruth (Baity) Mattingly marry? This is the direct link between the Baity and Mattingly lines and needs confirmation. Was it Leroy Teichmuller Mattingly (b. 1898)?
What is Shari's mother's maiden name? This would resolve the Frost connection definitively.

Who is the 107-year-old centenarian (b. ~1828, d. ~1935)? The Baity line has William D. Baity (b. 1829, d. 1894) — too early a death. The centenarian is on the Mattingly side. Census records 1850-1930 for Texas Mattinglys aged 70-107 would narrow this.

What is Ed Mattingly's full first name and his parentage? The 1902 Fayette County history confirms 'Ed. Mattingly' as city treasurer of La Grange and husband of May Teichmueller (the assistant cashier of First National Bank). His Mattingly ancestry going back to Maryland is the key missing link.
Was Paralee Johnson (Pearl Baity) born in North Carolina? Her father Morgan Johnson was in Denison TX by 1900; whether he migrated from NC is not confirmed by the WikiTree profile.
What are the three unnamed Teichmueller children? The 1902 history says Hans and Augusta had five children: May, Minetta, Anna, and two unnamed. Their names and fate would add to the tree.
The 'Omny Parish, Sussex' birthplace in WikiTree for Thomas Mattingly I — this likely means Horsham or nearby Sussex, not 'Omny' as a placename. Reconcile with the Mattingley Hampshire origin.
What was Thomas Claiborne Frost's birthplace and parentage? Confederate Army records at NARA and San Antonio genealogy sources would establish his origins.

Did Leonard Ignatius Mattingly (1704-1789) migrate to Kentucky in 1785, or did he stay in Maryland? This determines whether the Texas Mattingly line went through Kentucky or stayed in Maryland until the Texas migration.

Mattingly and Moore Distillery (Bardstown KY, founded 1877): the Wikipedia page exists but returned no text body in scraping. Confirm founders and their relation to the Maryland Catholic Mattingly line.
